topic: Someone's childhood imaginary friend appears in their niece's drawings
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor stared at the sheet of paper as if it were contaminated. The crayon lines depicted a creature with too many limbs and eyes that seemed to follow her movements, a caricature of the spectral companion she used to invent as a child. A cold knot tightened in her stomach. She picked up a tissue, rubbing the bridge of her nose vigorously, trying to clear the sudden, unwelcome image from her vision. It wasn't just strange; it was profoundly unsettling, a trespass into a space she had carefully walled off. She felt a distinct urge to crumple the drawing into a tight ball and throw it into the bin, a sharp, unpleasant tension radiating through her shoulders.

[story 2]

"Oh, look, Aunt Clara! He’s so silly!" Lily chirped, holding up the sketch with an unbridled enthusiasm that grated on Marcus's nerves. Marcus leaned back in his chair, his jaw clenching almost imperceptibly. The drawing was undeniably vivid—a grotesque amalgamation of shapes and unsettling expressions, clearly the product of some deeply private, perhaps disturbing, inner world. He traced the outline of one of the figures with a fingertip, then immediately pulled his hand back as if burned. A wave of profound unease washed over him, making him want to stand up and walk out of the room, to simply erase the entire endeavor from existence.

[story 3]

I watched my niece, Maya, meticulously color the page. The figure in the corner—a spindly thing with exaggerated features—was undeniably familiar, yet warped beyond recognition. Every time my gaze landed on it, a tightness seized my chest, making my breath catch in my throat. I shifted in my seat, the fabric of my sweater suddenly feeling scratchy against my skin. I forced a neutral expression, offering a weak, patronizing smile. "That's... certainly creative, sweetie," I managed, my voice sounding oddly strained, as if speaking required immense effort. I focused intently on a dust mote dancing in the sunlight, anything but the unsettling presence lurking on the paper.
